image.alt: Abstract modern skyscraper geometry photographed against a warm sky

situation.headline: The situation
paragraphs: A company founder held $22M in employer stock with a 12-month lock-up period. The position represented 85% of total net worth. A 30% decline in the stock price would have wiped out a decade of personal savings.
The founder wanted downside protection but was unwilling to forfeit all upside participation. Selling was not an option during the lock-up.

approach.headline: Our approach
paragraphs: We structured a costless collar using listed options on the underlying stock. The collar established a price floor at 15% below the current market value and capped upside participation at 25% above. No premium was required because the put and call values offset each other.

We also built a parallel diversified portfolio from the founder's remaining liquid assets. This portfolio was designed to perform independently of the concentrated stock, reducing overall household correlation to a single company.

outcome.headline: The outcome
paragraphs: During the lock-up, the underlying stock declined 18%. The collar's put option activated, and the founder's effective loss was limited to the 15% floor. Without the hedge, the unrealised loss would have exceeded $3.9M.

After the lock-up expired, we executed a staged diversification plan over six months. The concentrated position now represents under 20% of total holdings. The founder reviews the portfolio quarterly and has a written plan for any future equity grants.
